schools.summary: The names of Israel's house that came into Egypt: 'now these are the names of the children of Israel, which came into Egypt; every man and his household came with Jacob' — the opening of Exodus repeating the list of those who went down to Egypt (Genesis 46:8-27), binding Exodus to Genesis (the names of the tribal heads, the covenant family entering Egypt, the seventy souls, v.5, the foundation of the nation about to be born); each coming 'with his household' (with Jacob) — the whole covenant family removing to Egypt (Genesis 46:6-7, the household of faith, the seed from which the great nation would grow); the repeating of the names (Genesis 46) — Exodus continuing the story of Genesis (the providence over the covenant family, the seventy who went down now to multiply into a nation, v.7, the bridge from the patriarchs to the people) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: Now these are the names of the children of Israel, which came into Egypt; every man and his household came with Jacob
the names of Israel's house that came into Egypt
the opening of Exodus binding the book to Genesis (Genesis 46:8, 6-7)
the covenant family, the seed of the nation about to be born (v.7)

schools.summary: The first four sons of Jacob by Leah: 'Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah' — the first four sons of Jacob (by Leah) named: Reuben (the firstborn), Simeon, Levi, and Judah (the heads of four of the tribes, the genealogy, Genesis 46:8-15, the line of Leah's first sons, Levi the priestly tribe, Judah the royal and Messianic, Genesis 49:10); the first three of Leah (Reuben, Simeon, Levi) — the elder sons (the tribes of Israel, the genealogy); 'and Judah' (the fourth son, Leah's, the royal and Messianic tribe, Genesis 49:10, from which Christ would come, Matthew 1:2-3; Hebrews 7:14, the line of promise, the chief of the tribes) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah
the first four sons of Jacob by Leah
the tribal heads, Levi the priestly tribe (Genesis 46:8-15)
Judah the royal and Messianic tribe (Genesis 49:10; Hebrews 7:14)

schools.summary: Three more sons of Jacob: 'Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin' — three more sons of Jacob: Issachar and Zebulun (Leah's last two sons) and Benjamin (Rachel's younger son, the heads of three tribes, the genealogy, Genesis 46:13-14, 21, the continuing list); Issachar and Zebulun (the last of Leah's six sons) — the tribes (the genealogy); 'and Benjamin' (Rachel's younger son, Joseph being already in Egypt, v.5, the tribe of Benjamin, Genesis 46:21, the line of Rachel, the genealogy) — JFB, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin
three more sons of Jacob
Leah's last sons and Rachel's younger son (Genesis 46:13-14, 21)
the tribal heads

schools.summary: The sons of the handmaids: 'Dan, and Naphtali, Gad, and Asher' — the four sons of the handmaids: Dan and Naphtali (Bilhah's sons) and Gad and Asher (Zilpah's sons, the heads of four tribes, the genealogy, Genesis 46:16-25, the completing of the eleven sons named, Joseph already in Egypt, v.5); Dan and Naphtali (Bilhah's, Rachel's handmaid) — the tribes (the genealogy); 'Gad, and Asher' (Zilpah's, Leah's handmaid, sons, the tribes, Genesis 46:16, the handmaids' seed reckoned among the tribes, the genealogy complete, eleven named, Joseph in Egypt) — Gill.

themes: Dan, and Naphtali, Gad, and Asher
the sons of the handmaids
Bilhah's and Zilpah's sons (Genesis 46:16-25)
the handmaids' seed among the tribes

schools.summary: The seventy souls of Jacob's house: 'and all the souls that came out of the loins of Jacob were seventy souls: for Joseph was in Egypt already' — the house of Jacob that went down to Egypt numbering seventy (Genesis 46:27; Deuteronomy 10:22, the small seed from which the great nation would grow, v.7, the covenant family, the number of completeness); the seventy (the small number, Genesis 46:27) — the seed from which God would make a great nation (Genesis 46:3, the small beginning of the multitude, the stars of heaven, Deuteronomy 10:22, the providence multiplying the seed); 'for Joseph was in Egypt already' (Joseph, and his two sons, already in Egypt, not among those who 'came,' the providence, Joseph sent before, Genesis 45:5-8, the count completing the seventy, the saviour already there) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And all the souls that came out of the loins of Jacob were seventy souls: for Joseph was in Egypt already
the seventy souls of Jacob's house
the small seed from which God would make a great nation (Genesis 46:27; Deuteronomy 10:22)
Joseph already in Egypt, sent before (Genesis 45:5-8)

schools.summary: The death of Joseph's generation: 'and Joseph died, and all his brethren, and all that generation' — Joseph (Genesis 50:26), his brethren, and the whole generation that went down to Egypt dying (the passing of the patriarchal generation, the setting for the change of fortune, the new king, v.8, the close of one age and the dawn of the bondage); the death of Joseph and his brethren (Genesis 50:26) — the generation gone (the protectors of Israel passed, the way opened for the oppression, the new king knowing not Joseph, v.8); 'all that generation' (the whole generation dead, the transition from favor to bondage, the providence, the death of Joseph removing the shield, the affliction beginning, that the deliverance might come, the covenant kept, the dawn of the long bondage, foretold, Genesis 15:13) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Joseph died, and all his brethren, and all that generation
the death of Joseph's generation
the passing of the patriarchal age (Genesis 50:26)
the transition from favor to bondage (Genesis 15:13)

schools.summary: Israel's great increase fulfilling the promise: 'and the children of Israel were fruitful, and increased abundantly, and multiplied, and waxed exceeding mighty; and the land was filled with them' — Israel growing exceedingly in Egypt (the heaping of words, fruitful, increased, multiplied, mighty, marking the extraordinary, God-given increase, the fulfilling of the promise, Genesis 46:3, 'I will make of thee a great nation,' the covenant of multiplication, Genesis 12:2; 15:5; 17:6); the abundant increase (the swarming, teeming multiplication, like the increase of living creatures, Genesis 1:20-22) — the providence multiplying the seed (the covenant promise fulfilled, the small seed, seventy, v.5, becoming a multitude); 'the land was filled with them' (Goshen, and beyond, filled with Israelites, the great nation born in Egypt, the providence, Genesis 15:13-14, the increase that provoked the new king's fear, v.9, the covenant promise visibly fulfilled) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the children of Israel were fruitful, and increased abundantly, and multiplied, and waxed exceeding mighty; and the land was filled with them
Israel's great increase fulfilling the promise
the small seed (seventy, v.5) become a mighty nation (Genesis 46:3; 12:2; 15:5)
the covenant promise visibly fulfilled, the great nation born in Egypt

schools.summary: The new king who knew not Joseph: 'now there arose up a new king over Egypt, which knew not Joseph' — a new king (the Hebrew may mean a king of a new line or dynasty, 'arose,' rose up, perhaps a usurper or a new royal house) coming to power in Egypt (the change of rulers, the turning of Israel's fortunes, from favor under Joseph's Pharaoh to bondage under the new king, the setting of the oppression); the 'new king' likely of a new dynasty (the majority view, perhaps the change after the Hyksos, or a native dynasty hostile to the foreign-favoring past) — the new royal house (the change of policy toward Israel, the king who owed nothing to Joseph); 'which knew not Joseph' — the new king 'knew not' Joseph: either he was truly ignorant of Joseph's history (the deliverer of Egypt, Genesis 41, the long years and the new dynasty having effaced the memory) or, more pointedly, he 'knew not' in the sense of regarding not, acknowledging not (ignoring or refusing to honor) Joseph's services and the debt Egypt owed him (the base ingratitude of the world, forgetting its benefactors, Ecclesiastes 9:15, the providence turning the tide, the favor forgotten, the bondage begun, that God might in time deliver, fulfilling Genesis 15:13-14, the warning of how soon the world forgets); the new king's rise (and ignorance of Joseph) — the providence beginning the bondage foretold (Genesis 15:13, the affliction that would make Egypt hateful and Canaan dear, the turning of the tide that set the stage for the Exodus) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Henry, Wesley.

debates.question: In what sense did the new king 'know not Joseph'?

sides.position: Truly ignorant of Joseph's history - the long years and (likely) a new dynasty having effaced the memory of the deliverer of Egypt (Genesis 41)
More pointedly, regarding not / refusing to honor Joseph's services and the debt Egypt owed - the base ingratitude of the world that forgets its benefactors (Ecclesiastes 9:15)

themes: Now there arose up a new king over Egypt, which knew not Joseph
the new king who knew not Joseph (the crux of the turning tide)
the change of rulers (likely a new dynasty) turning favor to bondage
'knew not Joseph' - ignorance, or the world's ingratitude forgetting its benefactor (Ecclesiastes 9:15); the providence beginning the foretold affliction (Genesis 15:13-14)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's alarm at Israel's growth: 'and he said unto his people, Behold, the people of the children of Israel are more and mightier than we' — the new king, alarmed at Israel's great increase (v.7), warning his people that the Israelites had grown more numerous and mighty than the Egyptians (an exaggeration, or a fear of their strength in Goshen, the jealous alarm, the pretext for the oppression, vv.10-11); the king's fear (the Israelites grown so numerous and strong as to seem a threat) — the jealousy of the multiplying people (the alarm, real or pretended, that bred the policy of oppression); Pharaoh's words (the alarm at Israel's strength) — the pretext for the bondage (the fear of a fifth column, v.10, the providence, the very increase that fulfilled the promise, v.7, now made the occasion of affliction, that God might deliver, the scheming against the covenant people) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And he said unto his people, Behold, the people of the children of Israel are more and mightier than we
Pharaoh's alarm at Israel's growth
the jealous fear of the multiplying people (v.7)
the pretext for the bondage and oppression (vv.10-11)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's crafty policy against Israel: 'come on, let us deal wisely with them; lest they multiply, and it come to pass, that, when there falleth out any war, they join also unto our enemies, and fight against us, and so get them up out of the land' — Pharaoh proposing to 'deal wisely' (craftily, subtly) with Israel, to check their increase (the politic cunning, the worldly 'wisdom' against God's people, the scheme to oppress them, vv.11-14, the craft that God would turn to folly, 1 Corinthians 3:19, 'the wisdom of this world is foolishness with God'); Pharaoh's pretext — the fear that Israel, multiplying, might in war join Egypt's enemies and rise against them, or leave the land (the political fear, the multiplying foreigners a supposed danger, the pretext for the oppression); 'and so get them up out of the land' (Pharaoh fearing Israel would leave Egypt, depriving him of their labor, the irony, the very thing he feared, their going up, God would bring to pass in the Exodus, but by God's hand, not their flight, the providence, man's craft against God's purpose turned to folly, the oppression hastening the deliverance) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: Come on, let us deal wisely with them; lest they multiply... and so get them up out of the land
Pharaoh's crafty policy against Israel
the worldly wisdom against God's people, turned to folly (1 Corinthians 3:19)
the irony - the very thing he feared (their going up) God would bring to pass in the Exodus

schools.summary: Israel set to forced labor under taskmasters: 'therefore they did set over them taskmasters to afflict them with their burdens. And they built for Pharaoh treasure cities, Pithom and Raamses' — the Egyptians setting taskmasters (slave-drivers, overseers of forced labor) over Israel, to afflict them with hard burdens (the beginning of the bondage, the forced labor, the oppression foretold, Genesis 15:13, 'they shall afflict them four hundred years,' the cruel servitude); the affliction (the hard burdens, the forced labor designed to break their spirit and check their increase) — the cruelty of the bondage (the policy of oppression, the affliction of the covenant people); 'they built for Pharaoh treasure cities, Pithom and Raamses' (Israel forced to build store-cities, Pithom and Raamses, in the eastern Delta, for Pharaoh's provisions or arms, the hard labor in brick and building, Exodus 5:7-19, the bondage in Egypt, the iron furnace, Deuteronomy 4:20, the affliction that God would see and deliver from, Exodus 3:7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Therefore they did set over them taskmasters to afflict them with their burdens. And they built for Pharaoh treasure cities, Pithom and Raamses
Israel set to forced labor under taskmasters
the hard servitude foretold (Genesis 15:13), the iron furnace (Deuteronomy 4:20)
the affliction God would see and deliver from (Exodus 3:7)

schools.summary: Israel multiplying the more they were afflicted: 'but the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied and grew. And they were grieved because of the children of Israel' — the affliction, designed to check Israel's increase (v.10), having the contrary effect: the more they were oppressed, the more they multiplied and grew (the providence overruling the persecution, God blessing his people the more under affliction, the frustrating of Pharaoh's policy); the divine overruling — God's blessing on Israel making the affliction serve the very end it was meant to prevent (the increase, the providence that turns persecution to the growth of the church, the blood of the martyrs the seed of the church, the church often growing most under the cross, as the bush burned but was not consumed, Exodus 3:2, the lesson — man cannot frustrate God's purpose, the more the world afflicts God's people, the more, by God's blessing, they grow); 'they were grieved (or, were in dread, loathed) because of the children of Israel' (the Egyptians vexed, alarmed, and filled with loathing and dread at Israel's continued increase, their policy failing, the frustration of the oppressor, the providence, the increase that fed Pharaoh's fear and hardened his cruelty, vv.13-22, yet served God's purpose) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What does Israel's increase under affliction display?

sides.position: The providence overruling persecution - God blessing his people the more under the cross, the affliction serving the very end it meant to prevent (the church growing most under persecution, as the bush burned unconsumed, Exodus 3:2); man cannot frustrate God's purpose

themes: But the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied and grew. And they were grieved because of the children of Israel
Israel multiplying the more they were afflicted (the providence overruling)
God blessing his people the more under persecution (the church growing under the cross, as the bush unconsumed, Exodus 3:2)
man cannot frustrate God's purpose; the Egyptians vexed at the failing policy

schools.summary: The rigorous bondage of Israel: 'and the Egyptians made the children of Israel to serve with rigour' — the Egyptians, frustrated by Israel's increase (v.12), making them serve with rigour (harshness, cruelty, severity, the harder bondage, the increased oppression, the affliction deepening, the cruel servitude); the 'rigour' (the harsh, cruel, breaking servitude) — the increased severity (the oppressors hardening as their policy failed, the cruelty of the bondage, the affliction, the iron furnace, Deuteronomy 4:20); the rigorous service — the breaking labor (designed to crush Israel, the cruelty that cried to heaven, Exodus 3:7-9, the providence, the affliction that ripened the deliverance, the groaning that God would hear, Exodus 2:23-25) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And the Egyptians made the children of Israel to serve with rigour
the rigorous bondage of Israel
the harsh, breaking servitude (the iron furnace, Deuteronomy 4:20)
the cruelty that cried to heaven, ripening the deliverance (Exodus 3:7-9)

schools.summary: Israel's lives made bitter with hard bondage: 'and they made their lives bitter with hard bondage, in morter, and in brick, and in all manner of service in the field: all their service, wherein they made them serve, was with rigour' — the Egyptians making Israel's lives bitter with hard labor: in mortar and brick (the building of the cities, v.11, the brick-making, Exodus 5:7-19) and all field-service (the bitter bondage, the crushing toil, the bitterness, remembered in the bitter herbs of the Passover, Exodus 12:8); the hard labor (mortar, brick, field-work) — the varied, crushing servitude (the bondage in all things, the iron furnace, Deuteronomy 4:20); 'all their service, wherein they made them serve, was with rigour' (the whole servitude marked by rigour, harshness, cruelty, the bitter bondage, the affliction that God would see, Exodus 3:7, the providence, the bitterness that made the deliverance sweet, and Egypt hateful, the groaning that rose to God, Exodus 2:23-25) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And they made their lives bitter with hard bondage, in morter, and in brick, and in all manner of service in the field: all their service... was with rigour
Israel's lives made bitter with hard bondage
the crushing toil in mortar and brick (Exodus 5:7-19; the bitter herbs, Exodus 12:8)
the bitterness that rose as a groaning to God (Exodus 2:23-25; 3:7)

schools.summary: Pharaoh charging the Hebrew midwives: 'and the king of Egypt spake to the Hebrew midwives, of which the name of the one was Shiphrah, and the name of the other Puah' — Pharaoh, the labor-policy failing (v.12), turning to a more murderous plan: charging the Hebrew midwives (Shiphrah and Puah, perhaps the chief of the midwives, representing many) to kill the male children at birth (v.16, the new, secret cruelty, the attempt at genocide); the midwives (Hebrew women, or Egyptian midwives to the Hebrews, the names preserved, Shiphrah and Puah, honored for their fear of God, v.17) — the women charged with the cruel task (the chief midwives, over many, the instruments Pharaoh sought to use); Pharaoh's charge to the midwives (the secret murder of the male infants) — the escalation of the cruelty (from hard labor to infanticide, the providence, the plot that God would frustrate through the midwives' fear, vv.17-21, the rage of the dragon against the seed, a foreshadowing of Herod, Matthew 2:16, and the dragon, Revelation 12:4) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And the king of Egypt spake to the Hebrew midwives, of which the name of the one was Shiphrah, and the name of the other Puah
Pharaoh charging the Hebrew midwives
the escalation from hard labor to infanticide (v.16)
the rage against the seed (foreshadowing Herod, Matthew 2:16; the dragon, Revelation 12:4)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's order to kill the Hebrew male infants: 'and he said, When ye do the office of a midwife to the Hebrew women, and see them upon the stools; if it be a son, then ye shall kill him: but if it be a daughter, then she shall live' — Pharaoh ordering the midwives to kill the male Hebrew infants at birth (secretly, at the stools, making it seem a stillbirth), but to let the females live (the murderous, cunning policy, the males killed to destroy the nation's strength and future, the females spared, perhaps for slaves or wives to Egyptians, the genocide of the sons); the 'stools' (the birth-stool, or the two stones, the secret means of killing at the moment of birth) — the cunning of the murder (to seem natural, the cruel device); 'if it be a son, then ye shall kill him' (the killing of the males, the destruction of the nation's strength, the sparing of the females, the cunning genocide, the providence, the plot God would frustrate, vv.17-21, and overrule for the deliverer's birth, ch. 2, the rage against the seed, foreshadowing Herod, Matthew 2:16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And he said, When ye do the office of a midwife to the Hebrew women... if it be a son, then ye shall kill him: but if it be a daughter, then she shall live
Pharaoh's order to kill the Hebrew male infants
the cunning genocide - the males killed at birth to destroy the nation's strength
the plot God would frustrate and overrule for the deliverer's birth (ch. 2; Matthew 2:16)

schools.summary: The midwives fearing God rather than the king: 'but the midwives feared God, and did not as the king of Egypt commanded them, but saved the men children alive' — 'the midwives feared God' — the midwives, fearing God (more than the king), would not obey Pharaoh's murderous command (the fear of God the root of their refusal, the godly principle, the fear of God overruling the fear of man, the command of God above the command of the king, the conscience awed by God, the women who would not shed innocent blood, whatever the king commanded); 'did not as the king of Egypt commanded': the midwives refusing to obey the king's wicked order (the holy disobedience to an unlawful command, the principle, 'We ought to obey God rather than men,' Acts 5:29, the lawful refusal of a sinful command, the king's authority not extending to commanding murder, the conscientious resistance, the example of the godly, refusing the magistrate when he commands sin, Daniel 3:18; 6:10; Acts 4:19); 'but saved the men children alive' (the midwives saving the male infants, preserving them, contrary to the order, the saving of the children, and, among them, the future deliverers, the nation preserved, the providence, God using the women's fear to frustrate the genocide, and rewarding them, vv.20-21, the courage of faith) — Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill.

debates.question: On what ground did the midwives disobey the king?

sides.position: The fear of God overruling the fear of man - the refusal of a sinful command on the principle 'we ought to obey God rather than men' (Acts 5:29; Daniel 3:18); the king's authority not reaching to command murder, the courage of faith that saved the children and (under God) the nation

themes: But the midwives feared God, and did not as the king of Egypt commanded them, but saved the men children alive
the midwives fearing God rather than the king (the crux of holy disobedience)
the fear of God overruling the fear of man, the refusal of a sinful command (Acts 5:29; Daniel 3:18)
the king's authority not reaching to command murder, the courage of faith saving the children

schools.summary: Pharaoh questioning the midwives: 'and the king of Egypt called for the midwives, and said unto them, Why have ye done this thing, and have saved the men children alive?' — Pharaoh, finding his order disobeyed (the male children living, v.17), calling the midwives to account (the angry inquiry, the demand to know why they had saved the children, the confronting of the disobedient women); Pharaoh's demand (why the order was not carried out) — the king's displeasure (the calling to account, the danger to the midwives); 'have saved the men children alive' (the midwives' disobedience exposed, the children living, the king's anger, the providence, God protecting the midwives, who would answer, v.19, and be rewarded, vv.20-21, the failing of the murderous plot) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the king of Egypt called for the midwives, and said unto them, Why have ye done this thing, and have saved the men children alive?
Pharaoh questioning the midwives
the king's angry inquiry, the disobedience exposed (v.17)
the providence protecting the God-fearing women, the plot failing

schools.summary: The midwives' answer to Pharaoh: 'and the midwives said unto Pharaoh, Because the Hebrew women are not as the Egyptian women; for they are lively, and are delivered ere the midwives come in unto them' — the midwives answering that the Hebrew women, being vigorous (lively, hardy), gave birth quickly, before the midwives could come (the explanation given to Pharaoh, whether wholly true, or a partial excuse to cover their refusal, the answer that turned aside the king's wrath); the midwives' answer likely true in part (the Hebrew women, vigorous from labor, often did deliver quickly) — yet the witnesses noting that, if the midwives spoke not the whole truth (concealing their deliberate refusal), this was a fault (the Scripture commends their fear of God and saving of the children, not any dissembling, the lesson — the good they did, fearing God, saving life, was right and rewarded, vv.20-21, but any untruth was not to be imitated, the end not justifying a lie, the witnesses careful to distinguish the praised faith from any blamed deceit); the answer (whether true or partly excusing) turning aside Pharaoh's wrath (the providence protecting the midwives, and the children, the failing of the plot, God overruling to preserve his people) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

debates.question: Was the midwives' answer to Pharaoh wholly true?

sides.position: Likely true in part (the vigorous Hebrew women often delivered quickly) - but if it concealed their deliberate refusal, that dissembling was a fault not to be imitated; Scripture commends their fear of God and saving of the children, not any untruth (the end not justifying a lie)

themes: And the midwives said unto Pharaoh, Because the Hebrew women are not as the Egyptian women; for they are lively, and are delivered ere the midwives come in unto them
the midwives' answer to Pharaoh
the answer (true in part) that turned aside the king's wrath
the witnesses commend their fear of God, not any dissembling (the end not justifying a lie)

schools.summary: God rewarding the midwives: 'therefore God dealt well with the midwives: and the people multiplied, and waxed very mighty' — God rewarding the midwives (dealing kindly with them) for their fear of him and their saving of the children (the divine approval of their godly conduct, the reward of faith, the fear of God blessed, the providence honoring those who feared him); God's reward — not for any dissembling (v.19), but for their fear of God and their preserving of life (the divine commendation of their courage, the blessing on the God-fearing, Psalm 31:19, the reward, the building of their houses, v.21); 'the people multiplied, and waxed very mighty' (Israel, despite the plot, multiplying and growing mighty, the genocide frustrated, the nation preserved, the providence overruling, the plot failing, the people growing, the covenant promise advancing, the great nation, Genesis 46:3) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Therefore God dealt well with the midwives: and the people multiplied, and waxed very mighty
God rewarding the midwives
the reward of their fear of God and saving of life (not any dissembling; Psalm 31:19)
the genocide frustrated, the nation preserved and multiplied (Genesis 46:3)

schools.summary: God building the midwives' houses for their fear of him: 'and it came to pass, because the midwives feared God, that he made them houses' — the reason of the reward (v.20) restated: it was 'because the midwives feared God' that he blessed them (the fear of God the ground of the blessing, the divine approval of the godly fear, the root of their courage, the principle, God honors those who fear him, 1 Samuel 2:30); the fear of God (the principle of their conduct) was what God rewarded — not the manner of their answer (v.19), but the godly fear that saved the children (the fear of God blessed, the principle of the reward); 'he made them houses' (God 'made them houses,' built up their families, gave them households, posterity, prosperity, the 'house' a family, as 2 Samuel 7:11, the reward of a settled, blessed family, the blessing on the God-fearing, Psalm 112:1-2, the providence, God honoring those who feared him above the king, the building of their houses for their faith) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass, because the midwives feared God, that he made them houses
God building the midwives' houses for their fear of him
the fear of God the ground of the blessing (God honors his fearers, 1 Samuel 2:30)
'made them houses' - built up their families, the blessing on the God-fearing (2 Samuel 7:11; Psalm 112:1-2)

schools.summary: Pharaoh's open edict to drown the Hebrew sons: 'and Pharaoh charged all his people, saying, Every son that is born ye shall cast into the river, and every daughter ye shall save alive' — Pharaoh, the secret plot through the midwives having failed (vv.17-21), now issuing an open, public command to all his people: to cast every newborn Hebrew son into the river (the Nile, the escalation to open genocide, the whole nation made the instrument of murder, the desperate cruelty); the drowning of the sons (in the Nile, the river the Egyptians worshipped, now made an instrument of murder) — the open, savage genocide (the rage against the seed, foreshadowing Herod, Matthew 2:16; the dragon, Revelation 12:4, the cruelty at its height); 'every daughter ye shall save alive' (the females spared, for slaves, or wives to Egyptians, to absorb the nation, the cunning cruelty, yet the providence, the very river of death would, by God's hand, bear the deliverer Moses to safety, Exodus 2:3-10, the plot overruled for the deliverer's preservation, the rage that God would turn to the saving of the deliverer) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Pharaoh charged all his people, saying, Every son that is born ye shall cast into the river, and every daughter ye shall save alive
Pharaoh's open edict to drown the Hebrew sons
the escalation to open genocide, the whole nation made the instrument of murder
the river of death God would make bear the deliverer to safety (Exodus 2:3-10; Matthew 2:16)
